prompt: Stress-test this EXACT pricing ladder against real 2026 stated willingness-to-pay and competitor moves: free Scan, $750 one-time signed Readiness Report, $299/mo and $999/mo continuous, $25,000 flat Reviewed Attestation (named co-signer), +$10,000 Deep Red-Team. Find verbatim 2026 evidence (@handle+date or cited pages) of what AI startups actually pay / call a 'no-brainer' / call 'too expensive' at each of these price points. Specifically probe: (1) is $750 a frictionless card-swipe or should it be lower/higher; (2) does the $299->$999/mo step make sense; (3) is the $999/mo -> $25,000 jump a dangerous gap that needs an intermediate tier; (4) Van-Westendorp-style: what's 'too cheap to trust' and 'too expensive' for a signed readiness report. Give specific dollar evidence per point, a verdict on each anchor (hold / adjust + to what), and confidence.
content:
**Evidence from 2026 sources (X posts dated 2026 and web reports/articles) shows AI/security spend by startups and small teams clusters in these ranges for relevant services (compliance tools, audits/attestations/readiness reports, pentests, red teaming, continuous SOC/AI security monitoring):**[[1]](https://frameworksecurity.com/post/how-much-does-cybersecurity-really-cost)[[2]](https://bluefire-redteam.com/red-team-cost/)[[3]](https://www.buildmvpfast.com/blog/compliance-security-tools-ai-enterprises)[[4]](https://underdefense.com/blog/ai-soc-pricing-guide/)[[5]](https://cybersecurityswitzerland.com/research/ai-security-statistics-2026/)[[6]](https://deepstrike.io/blog/penetration-testing-cost)

- **One-time or low-commitment audits/readiness/compliance reports/attestations/pentests**: Often $5K–$25K+ per engagement (e.g., SOC 2/ISO-related $5K–$20K evidence packages or audits; basic pentests $5K–$20K small-scope; full readiness or validated assessments in the $10K–$40K range including prep). Vanta-style automated compliance starts ~$10K/year. EU AI Act compliance tools frequently cited at €50K+/year (explicitly called out as unaffordable for most startups).[[7]](https://x.com/polsia/status/2061817525705601326)[[8]](https://www.complyjet.com/blog/iso-27001-for-startups)
- **Continuous/subscription monitoring or security tools**: $10K–$100K+/year for startups/SMBs (basic compliance automation ~$3K–$12K/year entry; AI SOC/MDR $30K–$250K/year depending on scale/endpoints; continuous AI red teaming platforms $100K–$350K annually). Startups often default to cheap flat plans (e.g., ~$200/month AI tools) rather than metered/enterprise options.[[9]](https://x.com/Panthini_7/status/2059529672296415600)[[3]](https://www.buildmvpfast.com/blog/compliance-security-tools-ai-enterprises)[[4]](https://underdefense.com/blog/ai-soc-pricing-guide/)
- **Red teaming (one-time or add-on)**: Small-scope $10K–$20K; mid/advanced $15K–$60K+ (full adversary simulation higher). Continuous AI red teaming far higher.[[2]](https://bluefire-redteam.com/red-team-cost/)[[5]](https://cybersecurityswitzerland.com/research/ai-security-statistics-2026/)
- **"Too expensive" signals**: €50K+/year compliance tools for EU AI Act startups; general cybersecurity for small businesses/startups budgeted $10K–$100K/year total (higher end feels premium). "Checkbox" cheap options criticized for lacking depth.[[7]](https://x.com/polsia/status/2061817525705601326)[[1]](https://frameworksecurity.com/post/how-much-does-cybersecurity-really-cost)
- **"No-brainer"/worth it signals**: Flat cheap plans (e.g., $200/month AI tooling) widely adopted by startups; tools that deliver CI/CD audit trails or automated evidence without sales calls or legal teams; ROI tied to avoiding breaches (avg. breach cost millions) or winning enterprise deals.[[9]](https://x.com/Panthini_7/status/2059529672296415600)[[10]](https://www.penligent.ai/hackinglabs/the-2026-ultimate-guide-to-ai-penetration-testing-the-era-of-agentic-red-teaming/)
- No verbatim 2026 mentions of exactly $750, $299, $999, or the precise ladder in the results, but market rates provide clear anchors. No direct Van Westendorp studies surfaced for this exact category, but implied thresholds derive from the above spend patterns and complaints about low-value "cheap" options.

**Probes and verdicts (specific $ evidence + hold/adjust):**

1. **Is $750 a frictionless card-swipe or should it be lower/higher?**  
   Market one-time signed/attested readiness or basic audit-style reports start materially higher ($5K–$20K+ range for credible evidence packages; automated compliance ~$10K/year equivalents). €50K+/year tools called unaffordable, but sub-$1K–$2K one-offs are absent and would likely be viewed as suspiciously cheap (low trust/quality signal, consistent with complaints about "checkbox" options). $750 appears frictionless on paper for impulse buys but risks "too cheap to trust."  
   **Verdict: Adjust higher** (e.g., $2,000–$4,000 range for a named/signed report to align with credible one-time audit evidence pricing while remaining accessible). Hold only if positioned explicitly as lightweight lead-gen/scanner output. Confidence: medium (strong directional data on audit costs; limited exact one-time "readiness report" quotes).

2. **Does the $299 → $999/mo step make sense?**  
   $299/mo (~$3.6K/year) sits at the low end of continuous compliance/monitoring tools and aligns with startup willingness for flat cheap plans. $999/mo (~$12K/year) reaches mid-tier continuous services without hitting the $30K+ common for fuller AI SOC or monitoring. The ~3x step mirrors common SaaS tiering (basic vs. enhanced continuous) and fits startup budgets that avoid €50K+ or $100K+ annual commitments. No contradictory evidence.  
   **Verdict: Hold** (step is logical and evidence-supported for different commitment/scope levels). Confidence: high.

3. **Is the $999/mo → $25,000 jump a dangerous gap that needs an intermediate tier?**  
   Yes—$25K flat reviewed attestation (named co-signer) aligns well with one-time pentest/SOC2 evidence or attestation costs ($5K–$25K+ range). However, jumping from ~$12K/year continuous to a $25K one-time (or equivalent ~$2K+/mo annualized) creates a steep cliff; many continuous offerings land $30K–$100K+/year, and red team/add-ons are $10K–$60K. Startups and SMBs show sensitivity to large jumps, with complaints about unaffordable high tiers.[[7]](https://x.com/polsia/status/2061817525705601326)[[2]](https://bluefire-redteam.com/red-team-cost/)  
   **Verdict: Dangerous gap—add intermediate tier** (e.g., $5K–$12K annual/quarterly reviewed or enhanced continuous option, or $15K–$20K attestation variant). Confidence: high.

4. **Van Westendorp-style: what's 'too cheap to trust' and 'too expensive' for a signed readiness report?**  
   "Too cheap to trust": Likely <$1K–$2K (implied by absence of sub-$5K credible one-time signed/attested reports in market data; cheap options criticized as checkbox/low-value, with real spend starting $5K–$10K+ for quality evidence). "Too expensive": >$15K–$25K+ for a standalone one-time signed report (full audits/red teams often $10K–$60K but one-offs top out lower than continuous platforms at $100K+; €50K+ annual tools already flagged as startup-unaffordable). $750 falls into the "too cheap" zone per patterns; $25K attestation sits at the upper credible edge for one-time.[[1]](https://frameworksecurity.com/post/how-much-does-cybersecurity-really-cost)[[2]](https://bluefire-redteam.com/red-team-cost/)[[8]](https://www.complyjet.com/blog/iso-27001-for-startups)  
   **Verdict: Too cheap to trust ≈ <$1,500–$2,500; too expensive ≈ >$20K–$25K for one-time signed readiness.** Confidence: medium (inferred from spend distributions and complaints; no direct 4-question Van Westendorp data found).

**Overall ladder assessment**: Core anchors ($299/$999/mo continuous and $25K attestation + red-team add-on) are directionally reasonable against 2026 data, with the monthly step working well. Primary issues are the $750 entry point (too low) and the large gap to $25K (needs bridging). Adjust $750 upward and insert an intermediate tier for better conversion and perceived value. No 2026 X posts or pages directly quote this exact ladder, but the broader pricing ecology provides strong directional validation.
